title: Anthropic Pushes Back on Fable Shutdown as G7 Scrambles for AI Access Framework
subhead: Anthropic publicly disputes the US export-control directive that forced Fable 5 and Mythos 5 offline, while G7 leaders at Evian propose a 'trusted partners' framework to restore ally access. Meanwhile, the UK bans social media for under-16s, Singapore's AI-driven exports hit a 20-year high, and the EU finalizes its trade deal with Washington.

sections.heading: Anthropic Pushes Back: 'This Standard Would Halt All Frontier Deployments'
sections.body:
Anthropic published a detailed statement disputing the US government's export-control directive that forced it to disable Fable 5 and Mythos 5 worldwide on June 12. The company said the Commerce Department issued the order at 5:21 PM ET that day without providing specific details of the national security concern, and that the government's evidence amounts to a narrow, non-universal jailbreak — essentially asking the model to find bugs in a specific codebase — that produces findings 'widely available from other models,' including OpenAI's GPT-5.5.

Anthropic argued that no universal jailbreak has been found against Fable, and that the specific technique disclosed to the government identified only 'previously known, minor vulnerabilities.' The company warned that if the standard applied to Fable were applied across the industry, 'it would essentially halt all new model deployments for all frontier model providers.'

The directive applied the 'deemed export' doctrine — traditionally used for physical technology transfers to foreign nationals on US soil — to a commercially deployed AI inference API for the first time, as TechTimes reported. Because Anthropic cannot verify user nationality at scale in real time, the restriction aimed at foreign nationals became a global shutdown. European Parliament member Aura Salla responded that 'Europe cannot keep building its tech stack on access that can be switched off overnight by a foreign government.'
sections.whyItMatters: This is the first time the deemed export doctrine has been applied to a deployed AI model API, creating a new failure mode for every enterprise building on US frontier models: a Commerce Department letter can remove operational capability with no advance notice and no restoration timeline.

sections.heading: G7 Proposes 'Trusted Partners' AI Access Framework
sections.body:
G7 leaders at the Evian summit discussed a 'trusted partners' framework that could give approved countries and companies access to cutting-edge US AI models, including Anthropic's restricted systems. According to Reuters, three diplomatic sources said the proposal was raised during the summit's opening dinner, with US Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick participating in the talks.

French President Emmanuel Macron announced that Western democracies would establish a coordinated AI cooperation platform within one month, with a follow-up leaders meeting in September. Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei addressed world leaders — including President Trump — at a closed-door working lunch alongside OpenAI's Sam Altman and Google DeepMind's Demis Hassabis, calling for 'structured access to frontier models' and resisting the temptation to splinter the AI ecosystem along national lines.

The framework could include either countries or individual companies as trusted partners, Reuters reported, though eligibility requirements and technical safeguards have not been finalized. The EU is reportedly seeking access to Mythos specifically to study its capabilities and risks. A White House official said the administration 'maintains open communication with US allies' and remains focused on resolving national security concerns.
sections.whyItMatters: The proposal attempts to thread the needle between export controls and alliance cohesion, but no details on eligibility or safeguards exist yet. The one-month timeline for a platform announcement is ambitious given that Fable and Mythos remain offline and no technical mechanism exists for discriminated access.

sections.heading: UK Bans Social Media for Under-16s, Going Further Than Australia
sections.body:
The UK government announced a ban on social media for under-16s, following Australia's model but extending it further. The legislation will capture platforms including Snapchat, TikTok, YouTube, Instagram, Facebook, and X, while exempting messaging services like WhatsApp and Signal. AI 'romantic companion' chatbots will require a minimum age of 18.

Beyond the blanket ban, the UK is adding restrictions on livestreaming and stranger communication with children on gaming sites and other platforms — measures that go beyond what Australia implemented. These protections will default on for 16- and 17-year-olds to prevent a 'cliff edge' at 16. Prime Minister Keir Starmer said the government is 'going further than any country in the world.'

A government consultation received over 116,000 responses, with 9 in 10 parents supporting a ban and two-thirds of young people agreeing that under-16s should not use at least some platforms. Ofcom will conduct a rapid study on effective age assurance for verifying whether someone is over 16. The first regulations are expected to reach Parliament before Christmas and take effect in Spring 2027.

Big Tech pushed back: YouTube said blanket bans 'push kids toward anonymous, less safe services,' Meta argued they 'create new risks,' and Snapchat said disconnecting teens from their communities 'doesn't make them safer.' The government counters that the Online Safety Act's existing provisions already cover some harms, and that the new measures respond to how children actually experience harm online, not just where it happens.
sections.whyItMatters: The UK is now the second major democracy to legislate a social media age ban, and the first to add platform-level feature restrictions on top of it. The Spring 2027 timeline and Ofcom's age-assurance study will determine whether enforcement is practical or whether this replicates Australia's early implementation challenges.

sections.heading: Singapore's AI Export Surge Hits 20-Year High
sections.body:
Singapore's non-oil domestic exports (NODX) rose 38.4% year-on-year in May, the sharpest growth in over two decades, driven almost entirely by AI-related demand. Electronics exports alone surged 94.8%, following a 66.7% increase in April. OCBC chief economist Selena Ling called it the highest rise in 20 years.

Within electronics, integrated circuits rose 80.9%, disk media products jumped 227.8%, and PCs increased 140.9%. Nine of Singapore's top 10 export markets saw growth, with Taiwan (+135.2%), the US (+80.9%), and China (+31%) leading. Indonesia was the lone exception, contracting 26.9% on weak domestic demand and rupiah volatility.

eToro analyst Zavier Wong traced the surge to a 'single concentrated force': Microsoft, Alphabet, Meta, and Amazon collectively lifting their 2026 capital expenditure plans to more than US$700 billion. Singapore Semiconductor Industry Association executive director Ang Wee Seng noted that while not every dollar is AI-related, 'a significant portion of the electronics uplift is either directly or indirectly connected to AI demand.'

Analysts expect positive growth to continue but cautioned that 38.4% may be hard to sustain as base effects rise in the second half, with potential volatility from inventory cycles, geopolitics, and tariffs.
sections.whyItMatters: Singapore's export figures are a real-time barometer of hyperscaler AI infrastructure spending. The concentration of growth in ICs and storage hardware — and its direct link to four companies' capex decisions — shows how narrowly AI demand is channeled through a few supply chain nodes.

sections.heading: US Delays Blacklisting DeepSeek and 100+ Chinese Tech Firms
sections.body:
The US Commerce Department has held off on adding DeepSeek, CXMT, and more than 100 other Chinese tech firms to its Entity List, representing the longest gap in Entity List updates in over a decade — nearly eight months. The companies were already approved for the list by an interagency committee, but the Trump administration is deliberately delaying to avoid escalating tensions with Beijing during ongoing trade negotiations.

At least 75 of the delayed firms are connected to advanced semiconductor manufacturing, AI technology, and supply chains feeding China's military. DeepSeek has been accused of supporting Chinese military operations and evading export controls through shell companies to access restricted Nvidia chips. CXMT is China's most advanced domestic DRAM manufacturer, designated by the Pentagon as a Chinese military company, and under consideration for blacklisting over high-bandwidth memory technology used in AI training chips.

National security critics argue the freeze creates a window where US technology continues flowing to firms the government's own review process has flagged. The designations remain at the Commerce Department, ready to be activated if US-China relations shift.
sections.whyItMatters: The approved-but-unimplemented blacklists function as a regulatory overhang. Any shift in US-China relations could trigger rapid implementation, and companies with significant China exposure in semiconductors and AI should be stress-testing supply chains against that scenario.

sections.heading: EU Finalizes Trade Deal With the US as G7 Addresses Critical Minerals
sections.body:
The European Parliament approved the EU-US trade deal with 440 votes in favor, 151 against, and 50 abstentions, clearing the way for the agreement struck at Trump's Turnberry golf course last summer. The deal caps US tariffs on most EU exports at 15% and lowers duties on industrial and agricultural goods. EU member states are expected to give their final approval soon, ahead of a deadline set by President Trump that would have seen car tariffs increase.

Separately at the G7, leaders committed to reducing reliance on any single country for critical minerals, targeting under 60% dependency on any single non-G7 supplier by 2030, with an ambition to reach 50%. China currently mines roughly 70% of rare earths and holds 90% of processing capacity. The Trump administration is working with private industry on 'Project Vault,' a $10 billion stockpile funded through the US Export-Import Bank.

G7 members agreed to coordinate stockpiling, share information on systems, and launch an IEA-backed platform for critical minerals cooperation. They also committed to trace and track minerals through the supply chain starting with lithium and nickel, expanding to five new minerals each year. The American Action Forum noted that it takes 7-10 years for federal permits to open a US mine, with some studies showing nearly 30 years from discovery to production — a timeline that makes rapid supply chain diversification difficult without permitting reform.
sections.whyItMatters: The EU-US trade deal removes one source of transatlantic friction, but the critical minerals targets rely on permitting and processing capacity that doesn't exist yet. The 2030 goal of under 60% dependency is ambitious given current Chinese dominance and the multi-year timeline for new mines.

sections.heading: China's AI Price War Deepens as Capabilities Converge
sections.body:
A price war is accelerating across China's AI sector as narrowing capability gaps between models and falling service costs intensify competition. ByteDance launched Seedance 2.0 Mini at 23 yuan (US$3.40) per million tokens — half the standard version's price — following a promotional campaign for its Coze AI agent platform. Tencent cut prices for its Hy-MT2-Pro by nearly 70% on its TokenHub platform. MiniMax AI halved pricing for its M3 model series. Xiaomi slashed its MiMo V2.5 model by 99%, triggering follow-on cuts from other frontier labs. Alibaba offered 50% off its Qwen3.7-Max during the 618 midyear sales event.

Bank of America Securities analysts noted that China's AI landscape is 'vibrant and intensely competitive, with limited capability gaps across incumbents.' The commoditization pressure comes even as the US export-control regime tries to slow China's access to advanced training infrastructure.

Meanwhile, Hong Kong Science and Technology Park partnered with SenseTime to build a home-grown AI data centre by 2030, targeting 40,000 petaflops of computing power. SenseTime CEO Xu Li described a 'train on the mainland, process in Hong Kong, serve the world' model, positioning Hong Kong as a bridge between mainland Chinese AI capacity and international clients.
sections.whyItMatters: China's price war signals that model capabilities are converging faster than the US export-control regime can slow them, while Hong Kong's data centre plan reveals a deliberate architecture to route Chinese AI services through a jurisdiction with global financial access — a workaround that export controls weren't designed to address.

bottomLine: Anthropic's public pushback against the Fable 5 shutdown has shifted the debate from whether frontier models are dangerous to whether the deemed export doctrine is the right tool to control them — and the answer from both the company and European officials is increasingly no. The G7's 'trusted partners' framework is a diplomatic placeholder with no technical mechanism yet, while the EU-US trade deal and critical minerals targets address longer-term structural questions but don't change the immediate reality: a single Commerce Department letter can take a deployed AI system offline worldwide in hours, and no ally has a substitute. Singapore's export data confirms that hyperscaler capex is flowing into hardware at unprecedented scale, China's AI labs are commoditizing model access at a pace that outstrips US restrictions, and the UK just became the second major democracy to ban social media for minors — this time with platform-level feature restrictions that go beyond Australia's model.
sources: Anthropic, TechTimes, Channel News Asia, Reuters, OpenDataScience, JPost, Forbes, Snyk, MarkTechPost, UK gov.uk, TIME, E&E News, American Action Forum, Straits Times, Nikkei Asia, BusinessTimes Singapore, AsiaOne, CryptoBriefing, Cybernews, SCMP, WSJ, Politico, AFP
